debug_info.goal:
U V : Type
h : U ∼ V
⊢ Set U ∼ Set V

by
  classical
    -- Use classical logic to construct the bijection
    have h3 : Nonempty (U ≃ V) := by
      -- Extract the bijection from the type equivalence
      exact h
    -- Define the bijection between set U and set V using the bijection between U and V
    have h4 : Set U ≃ Set V := 
      Equiv.congr_arg (fun (α : Type) => Set α)
        (h3.some)
    -- Use the bijection to show that the sets are equivalent
    exact h4

by
  have h₁ : U ≃ V := h
  have h₂ : Set U ≃ Set V := by
    -- Use the fact that the power set of U is equivalent to the power set of V if U is equivalent to V
    have h₃ : U ≃ V := h₁
    -- Use the equivalence of U and V to show the equivalence of their power sets
    exact Set.equivSetOfEquiv h₃
  -- The equivalence of the power sets is exactly what we need
  exact h₂

by
  have h₁ : U ∼ V := h
  have h₂ : Set U ∼ Set V := by
    -- Use the fact that U and V are equivalent to show that their powersets are equivalent
    have h₃ : U ≃ V := by
      -- Convert the equivalence to a bijection
      exact h.equiv
    -- Use the bijection to construct an equivalence between the powersets
    have h₄ : Set U ≃ Set V := by
      -- Define the function that maps a subset of U to its image in V
      let f : Set U → Set V := fun s => Set.image (h₃) s
      -- Define the inverse function that maps a subset of V to its preimage in U
      let g : Set V → Set U := fun s => Set.preimage (h₃) s
      -- Prove that f is a bijection
      refine' ⟨f, g, _, _, _⟩ <;>
        (try simp [f, g]) <;>
        (try) {
          ext s
          simp [h₃.invFun_eq]
          <;>
          aesop
        } <;>
        (try) {
          ext s
          simp [h₃.left_inv]
          <;>
          aesop
        } <;>
        (try) {
          ext s
          simp [h₃.right_inv]
          <;>
          aesop
        }
    -- Use the equivalence to conclude the proof
    exact h₄
  -- Conclude the proof
  exact h₂

by
  have h1 : Set U ∼ Set V := by
    -- Use the fact that the bijection h between U and V induces a bijection between their power sets
    have h2 : Equiv (Set U) (Set V) := by
      -- Define the bijection between the power sets using h
      refine' { toFun := fun s => { x : V | (h.symm x) ∈ s }, invFun := fun s => { x : U | (h x) ∈ s },
        left_inv := _, right_inv := _, bijective := _ }
      · -- Prove that the composition is the identity on the left
        intro s
        ext x
        simp [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
        <;> aesop
      · -- Prove that the composition is the identity on the right
        intro s
        ext x
        simp [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
        <;> aesop
      · -- Prove that the function is bijective
        refine' ⟨_, _⟩
        · -- Injectivity
          intro s t hst
          ext x
          have h3 := hst
          simp only [Set.ext_iff] at h3
          have h4 := h3
          simp only [Set.mem_setOf_eq] at h4 ⊢
          have h5 := h4 (h x)
          simp [Equiv.apply_symm_apply] at h5 ⊢
          <;> tauto
        · -- Surjectivity
          intro t
          use {x : U | h x ∈ t}
          ext x
          simp [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
          <;> aesop
    -- Use the bijection to show that the power sets are equinumerous
    exact h2.equinum
  exact h1

by
  have h₁ : Set U ∼ Set V := by
    let f : Set U → Set V := fun s => Set.image (h.toFun) s
    let g : Set V → Set U := fun s => Set.image (h.symm.toFun) s
    have h₂ : g ∘ f = id := by
      funext s
      simp [f, g, Function.comp_apply, Set.ext_iff]
      <;> aesop
    have h₃ : f ∘ g = id := by
      funext s
      simp [f, g, Function.comp_apply, Set.ext_iff]
      <;> aesop
    refine' ⟨fun s t h => _ , fun s t h => _⟩ <;> simp_all [Set.ext_iff]
    <;> aesop
  exact h₁

    {

        intro s
        ext u
        simp [Set.mem_preimage, Set.mem_image]
        <;>

        (try

            constructor <;> intro h

        aesop
      right_inv := by
        intro t
        ext v
        simp [Set.mem_preimage, Set.mem_image]
        <;>
        (try aesop) <;>
        (try
          {
            constructor <;> intro h
            · obtain ⟨u, hu, rfl⟩ := h
              exact ⟨v, by simp_all [Equiv.apply_symm_apply]⟩
            · obtain ⟨v', hv', hv'_eq⟩ := h
              refine' ⟨h_equiv.symm v', _⟩
              <;> simp_all [Equiv.apply_symm_apply] <;> aesop
          }) <;>
        aesop
    }
  exact h_set_equiv

by
    classical
    -- Use the fact that the power sets of U and V are equinumerous if U and V are equinumerous.
    -- We can construct a bijection between the power sets using the bijection between U and V.
    -- Specifically, for any subset S of U, we map it to the subset of V whose elements are the images of the elements of S under the bijection between U and V.
    -- This mapping is a bijection between the power sets, hence Set U ∼ Set V.
    have h₁ : Set U ∼ Set V :=
      (Equiv.setOfEquiv fun h => h) (by exact h)
    exact h₁

  rcases h with ⟨f, g, h₁, h₂⟩
  have h₃ : Set U ∼ Set V :=
    Equiv.ofBijective (fun (s : Set U) : Set V => image f s) ⟨
      -- Prove injectivity
      fun s t hst => by
        have h₄ : image f s = image f t := hst
        have h₅ : s = t := by
          apply Set.ext
          intro u
          have h₆ : u ∈ s ↔ f u ∈ image f s := by
            simp [Set.mem_image]
            <;> aesop
          have h₇ : u ∈ t ↔ f u ∈ image f t := by
            simp [Set.mem_image]
            <;> aesop
          have h₈ : f u ∈ image f s ↔ f u ∈ image f t := by rw [h₄]
          simp_all [Set.mem_image]
          <;> aesop
        exact h₅
      ,
      -- Prove surjectivity
      fun t => by
        use g '' t
        ext v
        simp [Set.mem_image]
        <;>
        (try aesop) <;>
        (try {
          rintro ⟨u, hu, rfl⟩
          refine' ⟨_, ⟨g (f u), _, _⟩⟩ <;> simp_all [Function.rightInverse] <;> aesop
        }) <;>
        (try {
          rintro ⟨u, hu⟩
          refine' ⟨_, ⟨u, _, _⟩⟩ <;> simp_all [Function.rightInverse] <;> aesop
        })
    ⟩
  exact h₃
